item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a good SCP but you write a lot for o reason either way +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for all the kind words. I haven't done a lot of (creative) writing for an audience. It's nice to hear that it clicked with people. I'll have to see if there are any more ideas I can develop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shit man, you sure this is your first one? Cause, damn, this is actually really good. You really must've done your homework when making this one.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Well then I guess the Keter classification makes a bit more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, this is outstanding, especially for a first article! While it is true that we have plenty of parasitic anomalies, the execution makes it. When I saw you refer to the tongue-eating isopod and the brain picture, I started to figure out what was going on, but I was still pleasantly surprised by the level of detail you put into it. The ending with the subject trying to "complete" themselves, alongside the vivid imagery present throughout the entire article makes it really stand out.

I can't wait to see what else you make!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically competent but otherwise fairly standard execution of something we've got leagues of in the database. Not at all terrible, but not quite original enough for my tastes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought about this for a while. It's classified as Keter because it has not been contained, and cannot be at the present time. If/when it no longer exists in the wild, it would definitely be downgraded to Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a couple unnecessary uses of the blackbox, but otherwise it is a well-made article and a great beginning for you. +1

PS: ooh, I also think the object class should be Keter, not Euclid. Sure it is a dangerous entity with potential to cause mass chaos, but it seems to be east to contain - which is what the classes measure (containability not dangerousness). Ergo, Euclid is probably a more logical class for this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'll be able to stay here, secure and protected

heh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this is your first SCP… Damn… this is A great article… +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That image is a yuck, I'll say that much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP. Hopefully some of you enjoy it.

Every now and then, I become very aware of the existence of my brain. Just sitting back there. Soft, and fragile, and honestly not so well protected. It scares me a lot. That feeling was the initial inspiration for this story.

Public domain image from Flickr. Slightly edited.

edit: So, funny thing. When I was posting this I briefly considered naming the article "Ship of Theseus" but I thought, nah, too likely that someone used that one already. So then tonight I was browsing the index and noticed that somebody DID use that title before : SCP-150. Not one I'd read. I clicked on it and discovered that 1) it's a parasite, 2) it specifically references Cymothoa exigua, and 3) it includes (among other things) a person's brain being replaced without affecting consciousness. I've gotta say, this freaked me out a bit. I think mine is a fairly different approach to the idea, but wow.
